nativeRange.habitat: Demersal on sand or mud bottoms, generally at depths of 40 m and below in coastal Indo-West Pacific waters from the Red Sea and Persian Gulf eastward to the Philippines

diet.notes: The Orangebanded Stingfish is an ambush predator that has been reported to comb the substrate with its modified pectoral rays (finger-like appendages called dactyls) in search of worms, small shrimp, and small fishes. Newly imported specimens often refuse prepared foods and tend to be slow, deliberate feeders that are easily outcompeted at mealtimes. Most keepers report success by starting with live ghost shrimp and weaning the fish gradually onto frozen mysis or chopped silversides.

avoidWith: boisterous tankmates that outcompete for food

summary: The Orangebanded Stingfish is a small, highly venomous ambush predator from the Indo-West Pacific that buries itself in sand or mud and moves along the bottom on finger-like pectoral rays. Best kept solitary in a 200 L or larger tank with a deep, soft substrate. Spines require careful handling.
careNotes:
The Orangebanded Stingfish (Choridactylus multibarbus) — also sold under the name Bearded Ghoul — is a small but highly venomous ambush predator belonging to the stonefish family Synanceiidae. Some retailers and older references list it under the family Scorpaenidae, but current taxonomy places it in Synanceiidae.

This species spends most of its time buried in soft substrate with only its eyes and dorsal spines exposed, walking slowly on free, finger-like lower pectoral rays (dactyls) to comb the bottom for prey. A deep sand or mud bed is generally advised over bare-bottom or coarse-rubble setups, which do not suit its burrowing behaviour.

Venomous dorsal, pelvic, and anal spines deliver a sting that has been reported to cause severe pain and, less commonly, more serious systemic reactions. Bare-hand handling is strongly discouraged; aquarists are generally advised to use a rigid container rather than a net when moving the fish, as spines can become entangled in mesh.

Because this species tends to be a slow, deliberate feeder that is easily outcompeted at mealtimes, a species-only setup or a tank with carefully chosen, non-boisterous large tankmates is generally recommended to ensure it receives adequate food.
